Insider Trading.title: Theft, not fairness
Insider Trading.plain: The law doesn't ban trading on an informational edge — it bans stealing information that belongs to someone else.
Insider Trading.quote: “Insider trading,” I like to say, “is not about fairness, it’s about theft.” Lots of people trade securities with an information advantage ; you are very much allowed to trade while knowing things that other people don’t know.
Insider Trading.quote_title: You Can Insider Trade NFTs Now

Insider Trading.title: The tipping chain
Insider Trading.plain: Liability flows down through chains of people — the insider, the tipper, the tippee, and beyond.
Insider Trading.quote: The outsider (the “tippee”) traded on inside information that he got nefariously, and you (the “tipper”) gave him that information with nefarious intent. You got a “personal benefit,” in the language of the court cases, and so you are equally in trouble.

Insider Trading.title: Personal benefit
Insider Trading.plain: To convict a tipper, prosecutors must prove they got something in return — even just psychological satisfaction.
Insider Trading.quote: To convict a tipper, prosecutors must prove they got something in return — even just psychological satisfaction. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Insider Trading.title: Your own intentions
Insider Trading.plain: Trading on information you generated yourself — your own plans and decisions — is always legal.
Insider Trading.quote: You’re trading on your own intentions. That strikes me as the legal sort of commodity insider trading, though I should emphasize that we are in extremely uncharted waters here and I really have no idea.

Insider Trading.title: The 15 Laws
Insider Trading.plain: Matt's running catalogue of how insider traders get caught, formulated from years of SEC enforcement.
Insider Trading.quote: Of course if he had told her she might well have told him to knock it off, so really this is just a version of the First Law, which is: Don’t insider trade. (Also the Second Law: And not with the call options).
Insider Trading.quote_title: Insider Trading Is Not a Romantic Surprise

Insider Trading.title: Prediction markets
Insider Trading.plain: A new frontier where the same logic applies but the rules haven't fully caught up.
Insider Trading.quote: Other sports sometimes have stricter rules about insider betting, but not always. And people who set up prediction markets are more likely to argue that insider trading is good.
Insider Trading.quote_title: Insider Betting Can Make Things Weird

Options / Derivatives.title: Everything is secretly an option
Options / Derivatives.plain: Limit orders, executive pay, corporate bonds — once you see them as options, you can't unsee it.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Speed bumps The paradox at the heart of market structure is that “Standing Limit Orders Are Trading Options That Offer Liquidity.”[1] If a stock is trading at $100, and I place an order to buy it at $99.

Options / Derivatives.title: The Second Law
Options / Derivatives.plain: Don't buy short-dated out-of-the-money call options on merger targets. That's literally the first thing regulators check.
Options / Derivatives.quote: You can trade any instruments you want—buy short-dated out-of-the-money options, whatever—and don’t worry (for now!) about the legal issues.

Options / Derivatives.title: CDS and engineered defaults
Options / Derivatives.plain: Credit default swaps turned lawyers into traders — you profit when a company defaults, so some people started manufacturing defaults.
Options / Derivatives.quote: (There are variations, like paying the company extra to issue new bonds that will increase the CDS payoff. You can do that part without a manufactured default, or even without a default at all.

Options / Derivatives.title: Options in executive pay
Options / Derivatives.plain: Stock options as compensation are just call options on the company — the executive wins big if the stock rises, risks nothing if it falls.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Someone will pay you today for that uncertain future value—that’s how stock options work—so you can collect some cash.[5] This is all quite well known to hedge funds.

Options / Derivatives.title: Retail options and meme stocks
Options / Derivatives.plain: When retail traders buy calls en masse, market makers must buy the underlying stock to hedge, pushing prices up — a feedback loop.
Options / Derivatives.quote: [2] If I am a market maker, standing ready to buy or sell the stock, it’s even worse: When the stock is at $100, not only do I put in a bid to buy it at $99.99, I also put in an offer to sell it at $100.

Options / Derivatives.title: The volatility trade
Options / Derivatives.plain: Volatility itself can be bought and sold. Traders don't just bet on where a stock goes — they bet on how much it moves.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Volatility is up. There is an overhang of several very large rumored unicorn initial public offerings, most notably one from Uber Technologies Inc.
Options / Derivatives.quote_title: Direct Listings Are a Thing Now

Securities Fraud.title: Everything is securities fraud
Securities Fraud.plain: Matt's central shtick: almost anything a public company does wrong can be reframed as securities fraud by shareholders.
Securities Fraud.quote: Everything is securities fraud Is it securities fraud for a public company to have only one Black member of its board of directors? We talked last week about a shareholder lawsuit against Oracle Corp.
Securities Fraud.quote_title: They’re All Friends in the Index

Securities Fraud.title: The disclosure machine
Securities Fraud.plain: Companies must disclose material information. The question is always: what counts as material, and when?
Securities Fraud.quote: (I think that is kind of the point of public-company disclosure requirements?) Certainly they are taking more risk by buying illiquid pre-IPO shares than they would by buying in, or after, the IPO.
Securities Fraud.quote_title: Mutual Funds in the Enchanted Forest

Securities Fraud.title: 10b5-1 plans
Securities Fraud.plain: Executives set up pre-scheduled selling plans to trade legally even when they know things. Except the plans can be gamed.
Securities Fraud.quote: Executives set up pre-scheduled selling plans to trade legally even when they know things. Except the plans can be gamed. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Securities Fraud.title: The class action machine
Securities Fraud.plain: When a stock drops on bad news, a class action follows within days. It's almost automatic.
Securities Fraud.quote: I find that roughly 16% of securities class actions arise from conduct where the most direct victims are not shareholders. However,

Securities Fraud.title: Pump and dump
Securities Fraud.plain: Say something false to move a stock, then sell into the pop. Ancient crime, infinite modern variations.
Securities Fraud.quote: We talk about pump-and-dump schemes a lot around here, in which scammers buy stocks, put out fake but bullish reports and then dump the stocks. How do their reports move the stocks?
Securities Fraud.quote_title: Andrew Left Wasn’t Short for Long

Securities Fraud.title: CEO misconduct and stock sales
Securities Fraud.plain: If a CEO is hiding something material and sells stock, that sale itself becomes evidence of insider trading.
Securities Fraud.quote: If a CEO is hiding something material and sells stock, that sale itself becomes evidence of insider trading. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

M&A / Mergers.title: How deals get done
M&A / Mergers.plain: Investment banks run a structured process — the target, the bidder, the board, the fairness opinion, the deal premium.
M&A / Mergers.quote: ) Instead, the trading price of the stock before the deal was announced—$17.13 per share—was the best evidence of the fair price, so that’s what he gave the plaintiffs.

M&A / Mergers.title: Material adverse change
M&A / Mergers.plain: The clause that lets buyers walk away from signed deals — and the litigation that follows when they try.
M&A / Mergers.quote: With no financing contingency or diligence condition, the agreement gave Musk no out absent a Company Material Adverse Effect or a material covenant breach by Twitter. Musk had to try to conjure one of those.

M&A / Mergers.title: Activist shareholders
M&A / Mergers.plain: Hedge funds buy stakes and agitate for deals, spinoffs, or management changes — sometimes helpfully, sometimes destructively.
M&A / Mergers.quote: ” Carl Icahn didn’t like it, and sued, and is talking about a proxy fight. But when you read the “Background of the Merger,” you get a more sympathetic picture of this structure.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: You Can’t Vote on a Deal With No Deal

M&A / Mergers.title: Deal litigation
M&A / Mergers.plain: Almost every large acquisition triggers shareholder lawsuits. Delaware courts decide most of them.
M&A / Mergers.quote: Vintage, meanwhile, sued in Delaware Chancery Court. There was not much to say, but Vintage said it.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Don’t Forget to Do Your Merger

M&A / Mergers.title: Going private
M&A / Mergers.plain: Taking a public company private removes scrutiny and unlocks flexibility — if you can get shareholders to sell.
M&A / Mergers.quote: Would it have been able to go back to its private backers for $13 billion of capital after doing the largest leveraged buyout in history? Maybe, I don’t know; private markets were pretty generous for much of that time.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Elon Musk Had a Good Day

M&A / Mergers.title: Antitrust
M&A / Mergers.plain: Regulators can block deals they think harm competition. The standard keeps shifting with political winds.
M&A / Mergers.quote: They also agreed that, if the deal was terminated on the drop-dead date because they hadn’t gotten antitrust approval, Vintage would pay Rent-A-Center a $126.5 million breakup fee.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Don’t Forget to Do Your Merger

Bankruptcy.title: Chapter 11 basics
Bankruptcy.plain: Companies can restructure debts in bankruptcy while continuing to operate — a breathing space creditors don't get.
Bankruptcy.quote: In the case of Voyager, it filed for bankruptcy, and FTX offered to come in, move Voyager’s customers to FTX, and cash out anyone who wanted out. In either case the basic point was that FTX had enough money to cash out everyone, so no one needed to cash out .

Bankruptcy.title: Creditor hierarchy
Bankruptcy.plain: Who gets paid first? Secured lenders, then unsecured bondholders, then equity — usually in that order, but lawyers spend years fighting over the details.
Bankruptcy.quote: At this point, the normal outcome is that you file for bankruptcy and your creditors get your assets, which are worth less than the amount they were owed. But all these things are uncertain and decisions need to be made fast, so mistakes are made.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: FTX Is Still Looking for Money

Bankruptcy.title: FTX and crypto collapse
Bankruptcy.plain: SBF's exchange mixed customer funds with trading operations, creating a $8B hole. Matt saw the structure coming.
Bankruptcy.quote: On Thursday, FTX’s bankruptcy lawyers sued Mr. Kives’s firm, K5 Global, to recover the money that Mr.

Bankruptcy.title: WeWork's spectacular fall
Bankruptcy.plain: From $47 billion to bankruptcy in four years — the story of how narrative can substitute for economics, until it can't.
Bankruptcy.quote: Cole Capital We talked a couple of weeks ago about a fake press release from a fake company called “Cole Capital Funds” pretending it would buy WeWork Inc. for $9 per share.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: Fake WeWork Rescuer Was an Hour Late

Bankruptcy.title: Liability management
Bankruptcy.plain: Companies use complex debt exchanges to avoid bankruptcy — moving assets away from creditors in ways that are technically legal but deeply contentious.
Bankruptcy.quote: The two current trustees are both lawyers (as you’d expect of trustees), not oilmen or financial experts (as you might expect of oil-company directors).[4] There are no annual shareholder meetings, and there have been only four shareholder meetings in the last 30 years.

Bankruptcy.title: Distressed investing
Bankruptcy.plain: Buying the debt of struggling companies and profiting from reorganization — sometimes by steering the outcome.
Bankruptcy.quote: Much later, in 2017, a hedge fund called Aurelius Capital Management LP acquired some of those Windstream bonds and sued, claiming that Windstream had violated its bond indenture and was in default. Windstream fought back hard, but eventually Aurelius won.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: Maybe Companies Will Get Rid of CDS

Crypto / Blockchain.title: The basic innovation
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Crypto created a mechanism to produce artificial scarcity digitally — and built an entire financial system on top of it.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: Crypto created a mechanism to produce artificial scarcity digitally — and built an entire financial system on top of it. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Is it a security?
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: The SEC's central question for a decade: does a crypto token count as a security? The answer determines who regulates it.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: [2] And so the SEC decided that most actual ICO tokens are securities: They are used to raise money from investors with a promise of financial returns from a common enterprise, which is enough (under the “Howey test” of U.S.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Stablecoins
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Dollar-pegged tokens that promise $1 in, $1 out. The promise is harder to keep than it sounds.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: That cryptocurrency is a “stablecoin,” meaning that one Tether is supposed to be worth exactly one U.S.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Things Got Weird for Stablecoin Tether

Crypto / Blockchain.title: DeFi and smart contracts
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Finance without intermediaries — code executes automatically. But code has bugs, and bugs mean hacks.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: 00 per share to PSTH shareholders (“SPARs”) which can only be exercised after SPARC enters into a definitive agreement for its initial business combination. The SPARs are expected to trade on the NYSE and have a term of five years, subject to extension….
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Bill Ackman’s SPAC Will Be Three SPACs

Crypto / Blockchain.title: The pivot to blockchain
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: In 2017, tiny companies could boost their stock price 300% just by saying 'blockchain.' The SEC noticed.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: In fact, excluding secondary market transactions, as of today, Kin exceeds Ether and Bitcoin (which Director Hinman does not believe are securities) in daily blockchain activity, demonstrating Kin’s wide acceptance and adoption. (See https://blocktivity.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Kik Gets in Trouble Over Tokens

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Bitcoin treasury companies
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Companies that raise money to buy Bitcoin — a new financial vehicle Matt has watched with fascinated skepticism.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: MicroStrategy Inc. (now doing business as Strategy), the great financial innovator of our age, has delightfully solved both problems in the simplest possible way.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: Funding secured
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's August 2018 tweet that he was taking Tesla private at $420 'funding secured' cost him $20M and the SEC chairmanship.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: In August 2018, he tweeted “considering taking Tesla private at $420. Funding secured,” and spent a few days pretending it might happen.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: Elon Musk Had a Good Day

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: The Twitter acquisition
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk agreed to buy Twitter for $54.20/share, tried to walk away, got sued, and ended up owning it anyway.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: ” All of these problems mean that, if you are a Twitter shareholder and Musk says “I am going to buy Twitter for $54.20 per share,” you might not believe him. “Show me the money,” you might reasonably say.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: Musk's pay packages
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Tesla's CEO pay votes keep getting struck down by courts, approved by shareholders, struck down again — a loop.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: 20, and then he refused to pay and had no good reason for backing out of the deal, and the seller had to turn around and sell the thing to someone else for $25 instead, then the seller could go to court and demand that Musk pay the $29.20 difference.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: The Price of Not Buying Twitter

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: SpaceX and X.AI
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's private companies create constant related-party transaction headaches for Tesla's public shareholders.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: He once tweeted that the new Tesla Roadster would, um, fly: “Will use SpaceX cold gas thruster system with ultra high pressure air in a composite over-wrapped pressure vessel in place of the 2 rear seats.” I am genuinely uncertain how seriously that was meant.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: Don’t Take Elon Musk So Seriously

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: The Elon Markets Hypothesis
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Matt's formulation: markets now price the probability that Musk will do something irrational, because he often does.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: [4] So Tesla’s banks owned warrants to buy Tesla stock. One of those banks was JPMorgan Chase & Co.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: DOGE and government
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's government efficiency role creates novel securities law questions nobody has asked before.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: You can’t negotiate a deal and then show up at closing and Elon Musk turns out his pockets and is like “well I have like 69 bucks plus some Dogecoin.” You need to know if he can get the money!

Hedge Funds / PE.title: The 2-and-20 model
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: 2% annual management fee plus 20% of profits. Sounds reasonable until you do the math on a $10B fund in a flat year.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: 5% annual management fee on their assets, plus 20% of their gains. [3] In 2014, Ackman launched a new fund, Pershing Square Holdings (PSH).

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Private equity value creation
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: PE firms buy companies with debt, improve them (or just cut costs), then sell. The debate is which part actually creates value.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: Apollo If you are a private equity fund, you borrow a lot of money to buy a company, and then you try to operate the company in a way that makes enough money to pay back the debt and make you rich. Sometimes this works and everyone is happy.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Private Equity Looks Out for Itself

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Private credit
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Banks pulled back from lending, so private funds stepped in. Now they're so big they're starting to look like banks.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: You probably invest in other stuff — “credit,” corporate bonds and private credit and asset-backed securities — to earn a bit more yield. Much of this credit stuff, though, has shorter terms than 30 years; there is not that much 30-year corporate borrowing.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: People Are Worried About the Basis Trade

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Short sellers
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Betting a stock will fall — a profession that's highly regulated, often vilified, and sometimes very right.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: This creates a new set of investors who may be keen to sell -- or hedge their positions by short selling -- when the IPO finally happens. Short-selling of Lyft shares was particularly heavy after its debut.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Lyft and Uber’s Shareholders Were Strangers

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Archegos and hidden leverage
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Bill Hwang lost $30B in two days. The mechanism: total return swaps that let him hold enormous positions banks didn't know about.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: Archegos One important thing that investment banks do is lend money to hedge funds to buy stocks. This is risky: If the stocks go down, the hedge fund might not repay the loans, and then the bank might lose money.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Archegos Was Too Busy for Margin Calls

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Liquidity mismatch
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Funds promise investors they can redeem — but hold illiquid assets. When redemptions spike, the mismatch breaks things.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: ” Client redemptions brought assets under management to about $2.5 billion, “down from more than $6 billion a year ago” and $12 billion at its peak.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Losing Money Reduces the Risk of Too Much Money

Regulation / SEC.title: How enforcement actually works
Regulation / SEC.plain: The SEC brings civil cases for money penalties; the DOJ brings criminal cases for prison. Both need to prove the same underlying facts.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Securities and Exchange Commission enforcement action against Under Armour Inc. for “pulling forward” revenue.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: Under Armour Sold Some Clothes Early

Regulation / SEC.title: The revolving door
Regulation / SEC.plain: Regulators leave for law firms and banks, then come back as regulators. Matt treats this as structural, not scandalous.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Regulators leave for law firms and banks, then come back as regulators. Matt treats this as structural, not scandalous. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Regulation / SEC.title: Regulation FD
Regulation / SEC.plain: Companies must disclose material information to everyone at once — no more quiet calls with favored analysts.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Companies must disclose material information to everyone at once — no more quiet calls with favored analysts. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Regulation / SEC.title: Gensler's SEC
Regulation / SEC.plain: The 2021–2025 SEC was notably aggressive — crypto, climate disclosure, payment for order flow. Most rules got litigated.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Gary Gensler said a full ban on payment for order flow is “on the table.” Robinhood fell about 3.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: Does Robinhood Need Payment for Order Flow?

Regulation / SEC.title: When courts push back
Regulation / SEC.plain: Courts increasingly strike down SEC rules as overreach — limiting what regulations can actually stick.
Regulation / SEC.quote: We have talked about this case a few times, including when it was filed and when the lower court struck down the tariffs, and it has always struck me as quite straightforward. The United States has a Constitution, and the Constitution gives Congress, not the president, the power to impose tariffs.

Regulation / SEC.title: Payment for order flow
Regulation / SEC.plain: Robinhood gets paid by market makers to route orders to them. Is this a conflict of interest or just how markets work?
Regulation / SEC.quote: This is called “payment for order flow,” or PFOF. The intuition is that it is so profitable to trade with Robinhood customers that you can give the customers price improvement, and you can give Robinhood a check, and yet it is still a good business for you.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: The SEC Wants More Stock Auctions

Banking / Crises.title: Bank runs
Banking / Crises.plain: Banks borrow short (deposits) and lend long (loans). If depositors want their money back simultaneously, the math breaks.
Banking / Crises.quote: This problem is known as a “bank run” and is famous from, you know, the Diamond-Dybvig model that won a Nobel Prize last year, or from It’s a Wonderful Life . “The money’s not here.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: SVB Couldn’t Ignore Its Losses, But the Fed Can

Banking / Crises.title: SVB's duration mismatch
Banking / Crises.plain: Silicon Valley Bank bought long-duration bonds when rates were low. When rates rose, the bonds lost value — and word got out.
Banking / Crises.quote: It is a bit harder to say exactly what SVB’s bad decision was. A simple answer is “it made a huge bet on interest rates staying low, which most prudent banks would not have done, and it blew up.

Banking / Crises.title: Deposit insurance
Banking / Crises.plain: The $250K FDIC limit creates strange incentives — large depositors have reason to run faster than small ones.
Banking / Crises.quote: will contribute $5 billion of uninsured deposits each, while Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and Morgan Stanley will kick in $2.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: Big Banks Trust First Republic With Their Money

Banking / Crises.title: Credit Suisse's AT1 wipeout
Banking / Crises.plain: Switzerland's forced UBS merger wiped out $17B in Credit Suisse AT1 bonds while equity got something — the opposite of normal bankruptcy order.
Banking / Crises.quote: Firms led by Credit Suisse Group AG lost more than $500 million on the secured bond portion alone, Bloomberg reported. I mean it’s not that good a trade.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: Buyout Loans Have a Bad Week

Banking / Crises.title: Too big to fail
Banking / Crises.plain: Large banks get implicit government backing — which creates moral hazard. The 2010 Dodd-Frank rules tried to fix this; the debate continues.
Banking / Crises.quote: That some fund run by the biggest exchange would examine each firm’s secret balance sheets and decide who is worthy of a bailout? What if … what if there was some sort of decentralized solution?

Corporate Governance.title: Who actually controls a company
Corporate Governance.plain: The board is supposed to represent shareholders. In practice, management usually controls the board.
Corporate Governance.quote: The main perk of being the majority shareholder of a company is that you get to pick its board of directors. Zuckerberg is also, of course, the chief executive officer.

Corporate Governance.title: Dual-class shares
Corporate Governance.plain: Founders keep 10 votes per share, public shareholders get 1. Controversial but increasingly common in tech IPOs.
Corporate Governance.quote: Our high-vote stock will decrease from 20 votes per share to 10 votes per share. The voting power of our high-vote stock will sunset and automatically decrease to one vote per share in the event that Adam becomes permanently incapacitated or dies.
Corporate Governance.quote_title: We Is Sorry About the Weirdness

Corporate Governance.title: CEO pay
Corporate Governance.plain: Executive compensation is mostly stock — so CEOs are incentivized to care about the stock price above all else.
Corporate Governance.quote: Anything that shareholders have to vote on—director elections, executive compensation, etc.—Mark Zuckerberg can, and does, just decide by himself.
Corporate Governance.quote_title: Carl Icahn Wants an Oxydarko Vote

Corporate Governance.title: ESG and shareholder proposals
Corporate Governance.plain: Investors file proposals on environmental and social issues. Companies fight them. The SEC decides which ones go to a vote.
Corporate Governance.quote: ESL ESG I wrote the other day about how JPMorgan Chase & Co. got in some hot water for agreeing to finance the European Super League, the now apparently dead proposal to start a lucrative new league for the biggest European soccer clubs.

Corporate Governance.title: Activist investors
Corporate Governance.plain: Hedge funds buy a stake and demand change — new directors, spinoffs, share buybacks. Sometimes they're right.
Corporate Governance.quote: Activist investors are supposed to use the more detailed Schedule 13D. But Musk checked the passive investor box.

OpenAI / AI.title: The capped-profit structure
OpenAI / AI.plain: OpenAI was a nonprofit that created a 'capped-profit' subsidiary — investors could earn up to 100x but no more. Matt found this fascinating and slightly absurd.
OpenAI / AI.quote: All of OpenAI answers to its ultimate decision-making body, an independent nonprofit board of directors who do not own any equity in the OpenAI entities and who, broadly speaking, appoint themselves. They answer to their own consciences, not to any investors.

OpenAI / AI.title: The Altman firing and rehiring
OpenAI / AI.plain: OpenAI's board fired Sam Altman on a Friday, the company almost imploded over the weekend, he was back Monday. Corporate governance broke down in real time.
OpenAI / AI.quote: , and the nonprofit asserted itself dramatically last Friday when its board of directors fired its chief executive officer, Sam Altman, and threw everything into chaos. But for a moment ignore all of that and just think about OpenAI Inc.

OpenAI / AI.title: AI and financial markets
OpenAI / AI.plain: Can AI systems trade better than humans? And what happens to markets when everyone's AI is trained on the same data?
OpenAI / AI.quote: “The Nonprofit’s principal beneficiary is humanity, not OpenAI investors,” explains OpenAI. In the second diagram, I have written the word “MONEY” in large green letters.

OpenAI / AI.title: The GPU arms race
OpenAI / AI.plain: Building AI requires enormous capital for computing — creating an unprecedented financing challenge for young companies.
OpenAI / AI.quote: Maybe one day xAI will produce the only computer program that anyone uses, and will intermediate all of commerce, and will satisfy every human want, and will rule humanity with an iron fist. I mean!
OpenAI / AI.quote_title: Musk Has Money and xAI Wants Some

OpenAI / AI.title: OpenAI going public
OpenAI / AI.plain: Converting from nonprofit to for-profit to enable an IPO — a corporate structure transition with no good precedent.
OpenAI / AI.quote: Converting from nonprofit to for-profit to enable an IPO — a corporate structure transition with no good precedent. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Fed / Central Banks.title: How interest rates work
Fed / Central Banks.plain: The Fed sets the federal funds rate — the rate banks charge each other overnight. Everything else flows from that.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: If you buy a five-year $100 bond that pays interest of 8%, and then interest rates go up by one percentage point, your bond is only worth $96. As rates go up, the yield of your bond has to go up, and to make the yield go up the price has to drop.

Fed / Central Banks.title: Quantitative easing
Fed / Central Banks.plain: The Fed buys bonds to push money into the economy — and then has to sell them back when inflation rises.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: The firm’s balance sheet shows $200 worth of stuff. Somebody notices that the $200 worth of stuff is just a piece of paper with the words “this is worth $200” scrawled on it in crayon, and points that out online.

Fed / Central Banks.title: The yield curve
Fed / Central Banks.plain: Short rates above long rates signal a recession is coming. Except when it doesn't. Matt tracks this with measured skepticism.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: Short rates above long rates signal a recession is coming. Except when it doesn't. Matt tracks this with measured skepticism. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Fed / Central Banks.title: Fed independence
Fed / Central Banks.plain: Central bank independence from political pressure is a cornerstone of modern monetary policy — and increasingly contested.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: Central bank independence from political pressure is a cornerstone of modern monetary policy — and increasingly contested. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: How an IPO is priced
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Banks run a roadshow, collect orders from institutions, set a price. The first-day pop measures how much money was left on the table.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: 4% between the beginning of Uber’s roadshow and the time it priced, but it was up about 36.5% between December 2015, when Uber first sold shares for $48.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: SPACs
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Blank-check companies that raise money first, find a target later. The 2020-21 SPAC boom ended badly for most retail investors.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: Like most SPACs, DWAC has a time limit to complete its deal: Its corporate charter says that, if it has not completed a merger by Sept. 8 — next Thursday — it has to close up shop and return its money to shareholders.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: The Trump SPAC Needs More Time

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: Direct listings
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Companies go public without issuing new shares or using underwriters — skipping the banker fees and the artificial first-day pop.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: One approach is the direct listing, where you just list your stock for trading and the price sort of comes to whatever it is. Then, if you like the price, you can do a stock offering at the actual market price a few weeks later, why not?
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: You’ll Never Want the Roadshow to End

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: Lockup expirations
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Insiders can't sell for 180 days after an IPO. When lockups expire, the overhang of insider selling hits the stock.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: You can go public without voting stock, without a lockup, without an IPO at all. The fact is that a lot of corporate founders are pretty suspicious of the method that Wall Street uses to price IPOs.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: You’ll Never Want the Roadshow to End

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: The unicorn era
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Companies stayed private longer, raised at huge valuations, then went public into disappointment. Theranos was the extreme version.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: One thing that I have said a few times around here is that, while an IPO is traditionally a big milestone in a company’s life, that is just less true of giant tech unicorns like Uber. (My colleague Shira Ovide has made similar points.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: Uber Is Already a Little Public

Legal / Courts.title: Delaware and corporate law
Legal / Courts.plain: Most US companies are incorporated in Delaware, making its Court of Chancery the most important corporate law court in the world.
Legal / Courts.quote: sued Elon Musk in Delaware to hold him to his agreement to buy Twitter for $54.20 per sh Programming note: Lol .

Legal / Courts.title: How white-collar cases work
Legal / Courts.plain: The government needs a cooperating witness or documents. Wire fraud is the Swiss Army knife — almost anything can be charged as wire fraud.
Legal / Courts.quote: The government needs a cooperating witness or documents. Wire fraud is the Swiss Army knife — almost anything can be charged as wire fraud. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)

Legal / Courts.title: The Sarbanes-Oxley legacy
Legal / Courts.plain: Post-Enron law that made CEOs personally certify financial statements. The threat of prison changed executive behavior.
Legal / Courts.quote: with its financial statements and other information. It’s basically as though the foreign company itself issued stock in the U.

Legal / Courts.title: Spoofing and market manipulation
Legal / Courts.plain: Placing orders you intend to cancel to move the market, then trading against the movement. The line between strategy and crime is surprisingly thin.
Legal / Courts.quote: If these opportunistic strategies were, for instance, market manipulation, then presumably one of the regulators would have brought a case by now. But they haven’t.
